insights: All five agents handled exactly 100 incidents with identical closure counts, eliminating workload imbalance or absence-related assignment gaps as explanatory factors for performance differences.

Over half of all incidents (52.8%) exhibit negative resolution times due to 'opened_at' timestamps occurring after 'closed_at', confirming systemic timestamp corruption that fully invalidates duration-based performance evaluation.

Despite uniform incident volume and balanced priority/category distributions across agents, average resolution times vary significantly (2984–3800 hours), indicating the observed heterogeneity is an artifact of corrupted temporal data—not real operational differences.

The absence of any non-temporal proxy for agent absence, workload surge, or performance dip—combined with perfect assignment uniformity and no missing values in key fields—means no evidence supports the user’s core premise of agent-specific performance degradation.

Negative resolution times affect all agents proportionally and stem from inconsistent datetime logging (e.g., mixed nanosecond precision), confirming the issue is infrastructural—not behavioral—ruling out agent-level root cause investigation.

Incident priority and category distributions show only minor random variation across agents, definitively excluding assignment bias or complexity skew as drivers of apparent resolution time differences.
summary: The analysis conclusively refutes the user's core premise: there is no evidence of agent-specific performance dips, workload imbalances, or absence-related pressure, as all five agents handled identical incident volumes (100 each) with perfectly balanced priority and category assignments. The observed variation in resolution times is entirely undermined by pervasive timestamp corruption—52.8% of incidents have negative resolution times due to 'opened_at' > 'closed_at', a systemic data integrity failure unrelated to agent behavior. This invalidates all duration-based performance metrics and eliminates any reliable basis for diagnosing dips, absences, or workload management strategies tied to temporal performance. The next focus must shift to data pipeline validation—specifically auditing datetime capture logic, system clock synchronization, and logging consistency—before any operational performance analysis can proceed.
